A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''[[:en:Mitama|Mitama]]'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Camphora officinarum|Camphor]] [[:en:Sacred tree|Sacred tree]] with shrine at the base at [[:en:Kayashima Station|Kayashima Station]]]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old [[:en:Camphora officinarum|camphor]] growing in the middle of [[:en:Kayashima Station|Kayashima Station]]. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * [[:en:Fetishism|Fetishism]] * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * [[:en:Sacred tree|Sacred tree]] * [[:en:Tree of life|Tree of life]] * ''[[:en:Yorishiro|Yorishiro]]'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327532]] Wikidata name: shintai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

[[wiktionary:神体|神体]] [[wiktionary:御神体|御神体]] [[wiktionary:御霊代|御霊代]] [[en:Shintai]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[de:Shintai]] [[el:Σίνταϊ]] [[es:Shintai]] [[fa:شین‌تای]] [[fi:Shintai]] [[fr:Shintai]] [[hr:Shintai]] [[id:Shintai]] [[it:Shintai]] [[ja:神体]] [[jv:Shintai]] [[ko:신체 (신토)]] [[mk:Шинтаи]] [[pl:Go-shintai]] [[pt:Shintai]] [[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]] [[simple:Shintai]] [[sr:Шинтаи]] [[tr:Shintai]] [[uk:Сінтай (синто)]] [[zh:神體]] {{wikidata link|Q327532}} [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327532]] Wikidata name: shintai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q327532]] Wikidata name: shintai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]]
{{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism|1=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life|1=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]]
{{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix|1=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto|1=Shinto sects and schools}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic|1=Anthropomorphism}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai|1=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism|1=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life|1=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism|1=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life|1=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Shintai]][[el:Σίνταϊ]][[en:Shintai]][[es:Shintai]][[fa:شین‌تای]][[fi:Shintai]][[fr:Shintai]][[hr:Shintai]][[id:Shintai]][[ja:神体]][[jv:Shintai]][[ko:신체 (신토)]][[mk:Шинтаи]][[pl:Go-shintai]][[pt:Shintai]][[ru:Синтай (синтоизм)]][[simple:Shintai]][[sr:Шинтаи]][[tr:Shintai]][[uk:Сінтай (синто)]][[zh:神體]]
{{Short description|Objects worshipped at or near Shinto shrines}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Fuji_Kawaguchi_357.JPG|thumb|275 px|{{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} is Japan's most famous ''shintai''.]]
In Shinto, {{nihongo|'''''shintai'''''|||"body of the [[kami]]"}}, or {{nihongo|'''''go-shintai'''''|||"sacred body of the kami"}} when the honorific {{ill|Prefix|en|Prefix|WD=Q134830|lt=Prefix|1=Prefix}} ''go''- is used, are physical objects worshipped at or near {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s as repositories in which spirits or ''[[kami]]'' reside.<ref name="sos">''Shintai'', Encyclopedia of Shinto</ref> ''Shintai'' used in {{ill|Shinto sects and schools|en|Shinto sects and schools|WD=Q3952369|lt=Shrine Shinto|1=Shinto sects and schools}} (Jinja Shinto) can be also called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}shiro''|||"spirit replacement" or "substitute"}}.<ref name="sos"/>
In spite of what their name may suggest, ''shintai'' are not themselves part of ''kami'', but rather just temporary repositories which make them accessible to human beings for worship.<ref>Smyers, page 44</ref> ''Shintai'' are also of necessity ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'', that is objects by their very nature capable of attracting ''kami''.
==Description== The most common ''shintai'' are man-made objects like mirrors, swords, jewels (for example comma-shaped stones called ''[[magatama]])'', ''[[gohei]]'' (wands used during religious rites), and sculptures of ''kami'' called {{nihongo|''[[Cult image#Shinto|shinzō]]''|[[Wiktionary:神像|神像]]}},<ref>''Kami'' are as a rule not represented in {{ill|Anthropomorphism|en|Anthropomorphism|WD=Q132987|lt=anthropomorphic|1=Anthropomorphism}} or physical terms, however numerous paintings and statues representing them have appeared under Buddhist influence.</ref> but they can be also natural objects such as rocks ({{nihongo|''shinishi''|[[Wiktionary:神石|神石]]}}), mountains ({{nihongo|''shintai-zan''|[[Wiktionary:神体山|神体山]]}}), trees ({{nihongo|''shinboku''|[[Wiktionary:神木|神木]]}}), and {{ill|Waterfall|en|Waterfall|WD=Q34038|lt=Waterfall|1=Waterfall}}s ({{nihongo|''shintaki''|[[Wiktionary:神滝|神滝]]}})<ref name="sos"/> Before the forcible separation of ''kami'' and Buddhas of 1868 (''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'') a ''shintai'' could even be the statue of a Buddhist deity.
Famous ''shintai'' include the mirror [[Yata no Kagami]] (one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}}), {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, {{ill|Mount Nantai|en|Mount Nantai|WD=Q1964765|lt=Mount Nantai|1=Mount Nantai}}, the [[Nachi Falls]], and the [[Meoto Iwa]] rocks. Many mountains like {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|WD=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} or the Three Mountains of Kumano (Kumano sanzan) are considered ''shintai'' and are therefore called {{nihongo|''shintaizan''|神体山|extra=''shintai'' mountain}}.<ref name="ono">Ono, Woodard (2004:100)</ref> The most widely known and renowned ''shintai'' is {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}.<ref>For details on Mount Fuji worship, see [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=786 Fuji Shinkō], Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
A ''[[Makuuchi#Yokozuna|yokozuna]]'', a wrestler at the top of {{ill|Sumo|en|Sumo|WD=Q40561|lt=Sumo|1=Sumo}}'s power pyramid, is a living ''shintai''. For this reason, his waist is circled by a ''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|WD=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}'', a sacred rope which protects sacred objects from evil spirits. A [[kannushi]], that is, a Shinto priest, can become a living ''shintai'' when a [[kami]] enters his body during religious ceremonies.
The founding of a new shrine requires the presence of either a pre-existing, naturally occurring ''shintai'' (for example a rock or waterfall housing a local ''kami''), or of an artificial one, which must therefore be procured or made to the purpose. An example of the first case are the [[Nachi Falls]], worshiped at Hiryū Shrine near [[Kumano Nachi Taisha]] and believed to be inhabited by a ''kami'' called Hiryū [[Gongen]].<ref name="nachi">{{cite web|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/wakayama/rekishi/disco131.html|title=Hiryū Gongen|last=Kamizaka|first=Jirō|publisher=Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport - Kinki Regional Development Bureau|language=ja|access-date=28 March 2010}}</ref> In the second, the ''{{ill|Mitama|en|Mitama|WD=Q495568|lt=Mitama|1=Mitama}}'' (spirit) of a ''kami'' is divided in half through a process called ''[[kanjō]]'' and one of the halves is then stored in a ''yorishiro''. This is the process which has led to the creation of networks of shrines housing the same ''kami'', as for example the [[Hachiman shrine|Hachiman Shrine]], {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} or [[Kumano shrine]] networks. Because over the years the ''shintai'' is wrapped in more and more layers of precious cloth and stored in more and more boxes without being ever inspected, its exact identity may become forgotten.<ref name=bocking>{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto|year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5}}</ref> The first role of a shrine is to house and protect its ''shintai'' and the ''kami'' which inhabits it.<ref name="scheid1"/> If a shrine has more than one building, the one containing the ''shintai'' is called ''[[honden]]''; because it is meant for the exclusive use of the ''kami'', it is always closed to the public and is not used for prayer or religious ceremonies. The ''shintai'' leaves the ''honden'' only during [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] (''matsuri''), when it is put in a "divine palanquin" (''[[mikoshi]]'', a term usually translated in English as "portable shrine"<ref>Progressive English-Japanese/Japanese English Dictionary, 2008, Shogakukan</ref>), and carried around the streets among the faithful.<ref name="scheid1"/> The portable shrine is used to physically protect the ''shintai'' and to hide it from sight.<ref name="scheid1">{{cite web|url=http://www.univie.ac.at/rel_jap/bauten/schrein.htm|title=Schreine|last=Scheid, Bernhard|author-link=Bernhard Scheid|publisher=University of Vienna|language=de|access-date=27 March 2010}}.</ref>
== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
==See also== * {{ill|Fetishism|en|Fetishism|WD=Q182116|lt=Fetishism|1=Fetishism}} * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} * {{ill|Tree of life|en|Tree of life|WD=Q1651237|lt=Tree of life|1=Tree of life}} * ''{{ill|Yorishiro|en|Yorishiro|WD=Q333798|lt=Yorishiro|1=Yorishiro}}'' * Types of Shintai ** [[Iwakura rock]] ** [[Kannabi]] ** [[Shinboku]]
== Citations == {{Reflist}}
== General and cited references == {{refbegin}} * ''Encyclopedia of Shinto'', [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=292 Shintai], accessed on September 20, 2009. * {{cite book | last = Sokyo Ono | first = William Woodard | author-link = William Woodard | title = Shinto: The Kami Way | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | year = 2004 | isbn = 978-0-8048-3557-2 }} * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }} * {{cite book | title = Basic Terms of Shinto | publisher = Kokugakuin University, Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics | year = 1985 | location = Tokyo }} {{refend}}

== Examples == [[File:Kayashima shrine - panoramio (1).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=Camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} {{ill|Sacred tree|en|Sacred tree|WD=Q12373247|lt=Sacred tree|1=Sacred tree}} with shrine at the base at {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}]]
An example of the importance of a [[Shinboku|sacred tree]] is the 700-year-old {{ill|Camphora officinarum|en|Camphora officinarum|WD=Q158722|lt=camphor|1=Camphora officinarum}} growing in the middle of {{ill|Kayashima Station|en|Kayashima Station|WD=Q1185827|lt=Kayashima Station|1=Kayashima Station}}. Locals protested against moving the tree when the railway station had to be expanded, so the station was built around it.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://mymodernmet.com/kayashima-station-camphor-tree/|title = Japanese Train Station Protectively Built Around a 700-Year-Old Tree|date = 27 January 2017}}</ref>
